title: Converting Between Lists and Vectors
description: Using unlist() to flatten lists, and splitting vectors into lists.

slides.title: Flattening Lists with unlist() 🔗
slides.content:
`unlist()` converts a list to a vector by recursively concatenating all components. It attempts to coerce elements to a common type.

```r
l <- list(a = 1:3, b = 4:5, c = 6)
unlist(l)   # 1 2 3 4 5 6
```

Names may be created from the original component names and indices.

slides.title: unlist Example
slides.codeSnippet:
l <- list(x = 10, y = 20:22, z = 30)
u <- unlist(l)
print(u)
print(class(u))

quizData.question: What is the result of `unlist(list(a = c(TRUE, FALSE), b = 1:2))`?
quizData.explanation: Logical values are coerced to numeric (TRUE=1, FALSE=0) because numeric is more flexible. So result is 1,0,1,2.

slides.title: Splitting Vectors into Lists with split()
slides.content:
`split()` divides a vector into groups based on a factor and returns a list.

```r
x <- c(1,2,3,4,5,6)
g <- c("A","A","B","B","C","C")
split(x, g)   # list with components A, B, C
```

slides.title: split Example
slides.codeSnippet:
scores <- c(85, 92, 78, 88, 95)
grades <- c("B", "A", "C", "B", "A")
split(scores, grades)

slides.title: List to Data Frame?
slides.content:
While lists are flexible, many functions expect data frames. You can convert a list of equal-length vectors to a data frame using `as.data.frame()`.

```r
l <- list(name = c("Alice","Bob"), age = c(25,30))
df <- as.data.frame(l)
```

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: `unlist()` is handy for quickly converting a list to a vector, but be cautious with mixed types because coercion may change your data.
